As much as this game is a piece of history for the shooter genre as a whole it is extremely dated and doesn't feel great to play in my opinion. I'm going to first go into what I liked about the game and then break down what I didn't. As a side note I played and beat it on the hardest difficulty so some of what I say may not apply for lower difficulties.

-Starting strong, I really enjoyed how; for most of the challenges the game throws at you, there are multiple ways of dealing with them, whether they are intended or not. The freedom to choose how to deal with the situation allows you to be creative in your approach, which allows for a more enjoyable time all around. And if you're good at manipulating the game's physics engine it just opens more ways to play. 
-I also love how since the game is old and not very refined, a lot of unexpected things can happen involving entities. In my play-through I saw quite a few headcrabs clip a box I was pushing while lunging and just get catapulted, and it was amazing whenever they did.
-And lastly I enjoyed how I was allowed to murder all the scientists I wanted without worrying about the game really stopping me (most of the time). I had my friend watching me play since he loves the game and his reaction every-time I drew my crowbar when a scientist was talking was great.

I really struggled thinking of what I liked about the game but I wanted to at least include something so it didn't seem like I was just hating the game. I really came into it wanting to know why it was so well regarded. Now let's get on to the negatives.

-Most of the guns in the game are just bad, having either pitiful damage or horrible fire rate (Sometimes both) or ungodly spread. Reload speed is also a big problem I had, I believe it takes like 4-6 secondes to reload the crossbow and the shotgun reloads a single slug per second I think. It might be more like .7 but that's still pretty bad.
-Most enemy has their on bullshit that tends to just be annoying. Human enemies love spamming grenades, Vortigaunts like to hide in dark places and fire their hard hitting blasts at you that does 35-ish damage while you're trying too deal with another enemy and they'll just sometimes spawn in right behind you, the grunts have a homing weapon that fires slow so the tracking is really good and they always come is groups, bullsquids will just spit at you from across the map, and so on.
-As soon as you are spotted by the enemy they all know where you are and the human enemies have hit-scan weapons almost guaranteeing you'll take damage.
-The story is told through random npcs that you may just run by without realizing they were talking in the first place.
-Dialogue is delivered super flat paired with bad audio.
 -The bosses are barely bosses. Until you get to Xen they are literally just, lead boss from area A to area B and hit button. That's if you even decide to fight them because you can just run past most of the time.
-The movement is really floaty which is why you can abuse it so much, but for a casual player who isn't abusing it, it's just annoying and sometimes you'll just walk right off the ledge.
-For half the game you're inside a boring foundation that get's old real quick.
-And finally, the range on explosions are deceptively big, pair that with the human enemies spamming grenades it becomes a bad time.

I'm not saying don't ever play this game, I'm just saying I think people get to hung up on the nostalgia and claim it's better than it is. I'd have to give it like a 4.5/10 as an actual game.

This game is moderately fun. It's a really cool idea, but isn't executed too well. It is too fast paced which makes aiming good ricochets very hard, almost impossible. That causes people to not bother with the ricochets, turning the game into a weird but pretty standard shooter. If you want an old shooter game, there are a lot of much better alternatives to this. I'd only recommend getting it on sale as part of the Valve Complete Pack.

I appreciate that they had put more focus on the narrative. I think it complements the puzzles adequately, which if you have played the first game, are not really that interesting anymore, and it is really well executed. Unfortunately, the gameplay suffers from two aspects:

- Spot the wall: at around the middle of the game, way too many puzzles become "find the wall where you can place a portal". That's not engaging, it's a pain in the ass. It kills the pacing, and while it is used in big spaces that make you feel the fallen grandiosity of the labs, it just drags

- Overcomplication: where the original was very focused on exploiting interesting dynamics, this sequel, especially at the end, I feel abuses too much of stacking layers on layers of execution. Which means you aren't try to figure out what to do, but in which order to do it. I wouldn't say this is catastrophic, but the first one handled this one much more elegantly, if my memory serves, so had to point it out

If the story was something life-changing, then sure, I would recommend it despite the not-really-so-awful gameplay. It's not, though, it's just funny, so can't do that

Portal 2 isn't a bad game, and with sale prices so low it's probably worth the money. When viewed as the continuation of a classic, however, it falls flat.

In a single sentence, Portal 2 is more Portal, with more bits and bobs glued on. While the original was something completely new and brilliantly concise, this sequel is largely a rehash and introduces additional gameplay elements which feel unnecessary. Special mention must be made of the new "long range sniping" activity, which often means spotting small portal-able panels way off in the distance -- more tedious than fun. This game is certainly longer than its predecessor, but in a way that again evokes tedium more than joy.

The graphics and controls are fine. The audio is fine, though some of the computer voice lines are so distorted (and played back on top of other sound effects/voice lines) that it's hard to understand them. The story fleshes out the universe and has some good moments, but lacks the first game's pacing.

And that's it. Portal 2 is completely fine, it has no fatal flaws, but no one finished Portal and thought "I really hope some day there's a sequel which is completely fine and has no fatal flaws!"

Loving Half-Life 2 when I first played it, I was expecting to love HL1 as well. But it ended up as more of a love-hate relationship.

Starts out strong, with one of the more memorable intros in any video game I've played - with a few very fun and interesting levels with breakneck pacing and good environmental storytelling, a fantastic soundtrack, as well as genuinely frightening moments - but it falls apart very quickly. Gameplay eventually becomes a slog with mind-numbing repetition, particularly in Lambda Core and Xen. Boring puzzles, infuriating platforming, sections that drag on too long, you name it. By the end I was so fed up with it that I used cheats to finish Nihilanth because I just wanted it to be over already. And that's never happened to me before in any game I've played.

Maybe I'm being too hard on a game from 1998 but there are plenty of games that are older that I've enjoyed - even non-3D games. I don't know what it is about HL1 but it just isn't for me. If you like it, that's great. I can appreciate its influence on future games, too.

Maybe Black Mesa would fix the frustrations I had, I'm not sure. I guess I would recommend this if you absolutely must play every Half-Life game, but you aren't really missing much by skipping it either aside from the origins of a few things. Some enemies from HL2 are introduced in HL1, like the Headcrabs, Zombies and Vortigaunts but that's really it. I think you'll have a much more enjoyable time playing HL2 and its episodes first, and then play HL1 if you really [i]really[/i] want to. HL2 has better visuals, better levels, subtitles, more character interaction, and is all around a more accessible game.

[h1]TL;DR - Recommended?,[/h1] but the game hasn't aged well and Black Mesa might be a better way to experience it.

This game is disappointing. The story is funny, witty, and engaging, however the gameplay is weak. The first third of the game is made up of extremely simple puzzles with a couple more challenging ones at the end, then you have the middle third which is mostly going from place to place with very few simple puzzles in between, and the last part of the game has a couple clever puzzles but thats it. The puzzles here have only one solution - there is no room to be creative. There are very few tiles on which you can place portals - it feels very restrictive and simplistic. 

I was expecting the game to start simple, introducing you to concepts and then get more complex, tying the things you learned together and challenging you in creative ways. Thats not here at all. There are maybe 4 levels which are tricky and take some thinking, with the rest of the levels being simplistic and unrewarding.

Overall if you want an engaging story with witty writing, get this. If you are expecting clever puzzles which test your intelligence, and involve lots of interesting elements, dont bother. I wanted the latter and was sorely disappointed.

Half-Life was revolutionary back in the day, tying gameplay and narrative like no FPS before. But it has aged quite a bit: slippery movement (perhaps inherited from the Quake engine), glitchy physics, and the final levels are not too fun because they seemingly ran out of time to playtest it properly.

Now, it's still a good game that I would recommend in spite of the rough edges... if there wasn't a far superior alternative. The remake, Black Mesa, is so much better in every way, vastly polished and expanded and prettified, that I must recommend: if you (somehow) haven't played Half-Life yet, make that your first experience. Only then, optionally, play the original to see how far gaming tech has evolved.

When you read the book "Raising the Bar" about what it was like developing HL2, you will notice that the end product is very different from the one Valve planned from the start. Still, it managed to blow everything else at the time out of the water.  The intricacies, pains and lengths that the team went through is utterly astounding, making correct assumptions about player psychology, groundbreaking facial animations and also being the very first by doing away with cutscenes in favor of a more player-oriented approach.

The HL series will forever be my most favorite one. It is it's own category, exempt from and beyond any sort of Top 10- lists. I remember playing HL1 while I was 8 or so, and while it was scary, I knew this was something groundbreaking. Before HL, I had tried Commander Keen, Carmageddon 2, Duke Nukem and Wolfenstein 3D, so this was something I had never encountered before. I was a kid still back in 2004, and walking in to a cyber cafe to see that a sequel was out, I immediately sat down to try it. Again, it was mindblowing. The graphics were like magic, the physics something I didn't even think possible, but the most striking and personal one was the aesthetic and architecture.  I recognized it as very familiar somehow, familiar scenes I would find in my country walking down the streets or in residential areas, minus the alien architecture, Combine and aliens of course. The lead designer was Viktor Antonov, a Bulgarian. I don't know if it was in part because of the neighbouring countries had this same type of aesthetic or because Bulgaria was an ex-Soviet Union country, but as someone from an ex-Soviet Union country myself, it struck a chord with me. It felt like home. Sometimes when I get homesick, I just fire up HL2 again to walk down the streets of City 17 and hang around, watching how the calm, autumn sun's light reflects off the buildings. And then I start shooting metrocops.
Not only this, the game is riddled with tiny glimpses into the past, showing that the levels you go through had once served a purpose and were actually inhabitable.  It gives the game a sort of nostalgic but also lonely feel as you travel the outskirts of City 17. It has this tonal undercurrent that makes the levels more than just another area to shoot through.

The games we got are marvelous and iconic, with HL2 being the trendsetter for years to come with the aftershocks being felt to this day. With Episodes 1 and 2, we saw what Valve was capable of, and if you go through the Developer Commentaries you can see how everyone involved was utterly thrilled. They were talking about all of these new opportunities opening up for them, new technologies and techniques to really push the limits of games as we know them. The voice actor for Alyx, Merle Dandridge, talked openly about how the HL projects were not only her favourite ones, but also one of the more pivotal ones as she got to explore new aspects of herself that previous projects simply had no room for. HL changed the way she would do voice-acting from this point on. It is no exaggeration to say that she grew as a person and as a voice actor with these games.
I will go on to say that Breen's dialogs are among the best, and he makes for a compelling and believable villain. His voice in-game is still very haunting and grave depending on the situation, lending a very believable, very urgent and very threatening tone when you read between the lines of what he says and what he leaves unsaid. 

All-in-all, the HL series has been a very personal experience for me.  It was an experience that I still come back to time and again. The gameplay has aged like milk, but everything else still stands up, the most impressive things being the characters, animations, levels, puzzles, voice acting, characters and sound design. Which is to say, everything else that is not gameplay is timeless. HL2 managed to uphold my interest in the series for over 10 years, with the mother of all cliffhangers leaving me hungering for more. 


When this is all said and done, I am writing here as protest to the utter mishandling of the Half Life series. Instead of a glowing review, I have to "temper my heartfelt congratulations with a firm measure of disappointment." The list is so long that I really have to lend a moment to think where to begin. 

First of all, when EP2 came out, there were talks of an EP3, and it was a given that with this massive cliffhanger, the story must continue. Shortly afterwards, radio silence. No matter how much people badgered Valve, they weren't getting an inch. Anything that Valve said was very vague or very negative about the future of the series.  It dragged on and on and on, with scraps and bits of information gleaned year after year thanks to dedicated fans. Had HL ended with EP2, it would all be fine if a little sad to see it end so abruptly, but it would have closure. Instead, we were led to believe that an EP3 or HL3 was coming, and then suddenly any sort of discussion was stifled in the rudest and bluntest way possible, while not ever letting on if the series has truly ended. 

After years of hoping, we see the talent behind the games leaving. Antonov with his finely honed sense of architecture left, followed by the entire writing crew of Marc Laidlaw, Faliszek, Wolpaw, Kosmatka. Not only this, Breen's voice actor, Robert Culp, passed away. The hope of an EP3 grows ever distant, with Valve STILL being tight-lipped about everything.

Then we witness the biggest kick in the teeth, which shows Valve's utter contempt and apathy towards it's fans by revealing  that they are making a new game, which turns out is a Dota2 card game. 
 
And now, we come to the point where Laidlaw, after seeing time and again how the fanbase was so wrongfully abused like an unwanted child, gathered up his courage and rightly released a transcript of the story what would have been EP3, which itself didn't give any closure, but more of a continuation and yet another cliffhanger with which the remaining team could work off of after Laidlaw left. The people that helped prop up Valve in the first place were fans of the HL series, and they have been utterly neglected. I have no words for how badly Valve has treated their fans. Not only have they withheld information and have not even attempted to communicate, they haven't had the courage to come out and say just what exactly is the future of Half Life. Even saying upfront that it isn't coming is better than letting people make their own conclusions for literally TEN YEARS.

Valve has dangled the possibility of a HL3 infront of abused and desperate fans by not saying anything. I won't say that I didn't see this coming, I had braced for dissapointment the moment Valve grew silent. Now that Valve is big enough to live off people playing Dota2 and CS:GO, they can effectively cut off their oldest fans, chalking them up as nothing but acceptable losses. The fiasco that was paid mods, and the audacity of which it was trying to be pushed though is still a searing beacon of anger in my mind. It also speaks volumes that Valve is no longer setting the trends, but chasing after them with half-hearted attempts like Artifact.

It is exactly what Valve leaves unsaid that speaks the loudest. Valve is saying that they have given up. They no longer care for anything but how to squeeze the last cent out of you by investing into lootboxes and cardgames. How very fitting that the thing that elevated Valve to such heights would also be the thing that damned them. 

And yet still, after all this turbulence, Valve hasn't uttered a word. 

Valve has undermined the work and dedication of so many people involved in the project, abused the legions of loyal fans that stuck with them for over 10 years, and finally, have betrayed the very core tenents of what it means to be Valve. 

I am no longer the innocent, wide-eyed child I once was. I have learned not to believe in dreams anymore.

This game was on my wishlist ever since I played the original Half Life, because I thought "Hey, Half Life with better graphics and physics? Yes please!". At that point, I had already played Black Mesa, the fan-made mod for HL2 which is essentially Half Life with better graphics, better than this even!

Anyway, the Negatives:
- I had some graphical issues related to the factory levels with the conveyor belts, when I turned my flashlight on and look at water, the whole screen would mess up
- Gonarch at two points refused to move, meaning I had to skip the entire chapter (but I could do that easily via the menu, I didn't need to use commands!)
- Even enemies refused to move sometimes
- Xen in general was pretty broken and messy, so if that's your favorite chapter, be warned!
- You can't gib ragdolls by hitting them anymore, nor can you move them around like in Half Life 2

The Positives:
+ The graphics and physics are really nice, everything feels more real than the original
+ Destroying shelves and boxes and whatnot is way better and more fun
+ The Flashlight was improved a LOT, as well as lighting
+ Unlike Black Mesa, all the levels were exactly the same and the enemies behaved the same way as they did in HL1
+ The Menu was improved a lot, as well as saving and loading and the whole GUI
+ Movement is still the same, but it felt smoother to me and less clunky than in HL1

All in all, I'd only recommend this game if you have played Half Life 1 already and want to play it with slightly better graphics. I wouldn't recommend it over Black Mesa if you want a Half Life game JUST for the graphics, only if you prefer the gameplay and feel of the old game and want to see it with a slight twist!

What is Half Life: blue shift
Half life: blue shift is a standalone expansion pack for Half Life. Half life: blue shift was originally meant to be part of the Dreamcast version of Half life just like how Decay is for the PlayStation 2 version but unfortunately before the production of the Dreamcast version began Sega announced that they would be discontinuing the Dreamcast, so Gearbox ported Blue shift over to the PC. Half life: blue shift also was the first expansion to include the HD content which was meant to be for the Dreamcast version originally. What the HD content did was that it replaced things such as the MP5 for a higher quality assault rifle and it replaced some NPC models as well, in my opinion i don't really like the HD models so i have turned them off in the options menu.
The story
In Half life: blue shift you play as a security guard called Barney Calhoun who was later used has a main character in Half Life 2. Before the Black Mesa incident Barneys job was to protect the equipment and personnel within area 3 of Black Mesa but when the incident happens your main goal is to escape the facility.

The pros of Half life: blue shift
- Good story that fills in the gap of the Half life story line
- The game has some very good level design
- The AI was very good for its time and still is since they react to different thing such as if you wait near a door the marines will throw in grenades
- You have to interact with NPC's in order to progress through game, this is good because your not just opening doors like you did in the original Half life
- There are decent puzzles and they evenly spaced out in the game
- The game is very enjoyable including the combat even though enemy's can seem like they can take several rounds in the head

What is bad about Half life: blue shift
- One of the most obvious things is that nothing new was really added to the game such as weapons, enemy's etcetera
- The game is very short only taking me around 3 hours to complete and that was without doing the tutorial section of  the game
- I found that the game was easy even though i was playing on hard
- some times i got stuck on the environment such as in between boxes or on the walls located in Xen

Overall Half life: Blue shift is a very good game but does have a few issues but they don't affect the game too much since the game is very enjoyable, also even though the game is very short it is also very cheap so you do get what you pay for. I do recommend getting this game if you like any Half life game or just generally like old school first person shooters like doom, but if that is the case then you would of most likely would of played a Half life game.

[h1] An Appetizer [/h1]

I was thinking of a title to give this review that perfectly encapsulated what Blue Shift is and this was the best I could come up with...an appetizer. Compared to Opposing Force, this expansion pack is relatively short and can take you about three hours at most to complete. Moreover, there isn't anything new this game brings to the table other than showing how Barney escaped from Black Mesa. However, it is important to add that Blue Shift was originally going to be an exclusive addon for the cancelled Dreamcast port of Half Life which could help explain it's brief runtime.

In short, you're not going to lose much if you buy this game (especially when it goes on sale which happens quite frequently) but you're not going to miss out on much either if you don't. If you need a quick fix of Half Life but don't want to commit to several hours of gameplay, then Blue Shift is definitely for you. :)

I have a whole hell of a lot to say about this game. Is it good? Yes, but it's got some issues. This game started out perfectly fine, and I had written a positive review with only about 3 hours on my belt, and it was pure fun. now with a solid 13 hours and a good amount of experience with bugs, I still enjoy the game, but I must insist if you want clean pure nostalgia and don't want something that goes as far as Black Mesa, play the original, this is fine, and has a lot of added benefits that are to be expected with an already great game being ported to the Source Engine. But that's all it is, a port, not that that's a bad thing, but the only thing this game has that stands out from the original is some of it's game breaking graphics glitches. At points it was so incredibly bad that my entire screen had been blocked out by glitched textures. And man, oh man The Gorge, the absolute worst part of this game. Now the section itself is fine, but at one point a Helicopter flies in and starts trying to nuke your ass like the US on Hiroshima, you have to shoot the thing with an RPG you've just found, only problem? The thing's hitbox is so small it's practically begging for you to make a dick joke about it. Now after a solid 45 minutes of shooting and loading my save, I finally gave up, went into God Mode and progressed to the next area. After that horrible experience, I've since experienced no more than the occasional graphics glitch. Overall, should you buy this game? If you can sit through a couple of lousy hitboxes, and graphics glitches, then go ahead. It's a pretty good game, I mean how bad can a port of the original be anyways?

There isn't much of a difference between this game and the original Half-Life. If you're deciding which to buy, it honestly doesn't matter. The water is nicer in this game and there are ragdolls and better physics. Other than that, it's essentially the same game. Don't worry about not getting the "pure" experience of HL1 because this is essentially it. Lower resolution water and people who just lay down and die without leaving a ragdoll for you to smack with a crowbar weren't the cornerstones of the Half-Life. Playing HL is an experience, regardless of trivialities such as this.

I wouldn't get this by itself personally; if you really wanna pick it up, get it via one of the packs in the Store and/or wait for a sale.

One interesting note though, you can load up the content from this game in Garry's Mod. If you have Gmod, this might give you some fun stuff to play around with.

Counter-Strike 2 Review: Cheaters Ruin the Experience

Counter-Strike 2 had so much potential to be a groundbreaking update to a beloved classic, but unfortunately, the rampant cheating has severely undermined the experience. While the visuals and gameplay mechanics are impressive, none of that matters when cheaters dominate so many matches.  
It’s frustrating to invest time and effort into honing your skills, only to be repeatedly outplayed by hackers using wallhacks, aimbots, and other exploits. The lack of an effective anti-cheat system makes the problem even worse, as it feels like Valve is failing to address a critical issue for its competitive community.  
What’s even more disheartening is how this problem impacts both casual and ranked modes. Players who just want to enjoy a fair game are often forced to deal with blatant cheaters, and this ruins the fun and competitiveness that Counter-Strike is supposed to offer.  
While the core of Counter-Strike 2 has a lot of promise, it’s hard to recommend the game in its current state. Until Valve takes stronger action against cheaters and implements a robust anti-cheat system, the frustration will continue to outweigh the enjoyment. For now, I can only hope for better solutions in the future.

So here are the pros and cons I've seen throughout playing this.

Pros:

There are moments in some servers where Team Based are really Team Based, where you work together with your team whether to get the objective in the enemy base, or grab the objective from the enemy base to your base.

Mods as usual has some fun stuff, I had a blast playing them and it has its moments when that server has people and sometimes its dead. But when theres people, theres the fun. Preferably the mod maps were alot of fun.

Cons:

Unless you love Bhopping, or just want to get into it to make the game balanced. Go for it. To me, its what makes it boring. But nonetheless I still come back and play it.

If you are not, just hope you get good teams or for the very least are good enough to hunt down these bhoppers. Deathmatch Classic ─ a love story.

There it was. The last DMC server on earth. With last two players.
They were fighting to win. One versus one. Head to head.
They were winning their own small victories. Their score was close, they were the best.
And after playing for so long they grew attached to each other.
The player one really started to like player two, but he didn't know how to express his feelings.
After 3 kills in the row, player one decided to write a message in chat: "Hey, you're a really good player!"
To which he recived no answer. He waited. He though to himself - "Maybe player two dosen't know how to use the chat... or maybe they didn't see it, or or they migh not want to reply?" The player one felt empty. He never got an answer from his opponent which he liked so much.
So he just though "I'm just gonna play and beat the player two (the player two was 3 frags ahead.) and then we'll see if he wants to talk to me..."
After hours of fragging the player one was really attached to played two, infact he loved him. But after a while player one though "Thats it! I have been playing for too long, I should go get some food." Before leaving he though "I'll just add player two into my friends list, maybe we will be able to play together later."
But then the unpredicted happened.
He was about to add player two to his friends list, but he couldn't find him. Not on "currently playing with" nor in steam community search box. But then he realisted the reason the player two didn't respond, the reason he could not find him anywhere. The reason was that the player two was actually a bot. A simple AI creature made by server owner to keep the server alive.
The player one was shattered into pieces. The person he loved, the person he was so attached to. It was only a bot. A soulless creature that had one goal ─ kill.
He left the server in anger and uninstalled DMC.
So there it was the last DMC player had left the game. Forever.
The game was empty with one server and a bot. A bot running around looking for something to kill, but he could not satisfy his needs, he could not kill anyone, because nobody was there. The bot was frogotten as the game.
So there he was. Alone.

The Banach–Tarski paradox is a theorem in set-theoretic geometry, which states the following: Given a solid ball in three-dimensional space, there exists a decomposition of the ball into a finite number of disjoint subsets, which can then be put back together in a different way to yield two identical copies of the original ball. Indeed, the reassembly process involves only moving the pieces around and rotating them without changing their shape. However, the pieces themselves are not "solids" in the usual sense, but infinite scatterings of points. The reconstruction can work with as few as five pieces.

An alternative form of the theorem states that given any two "reasonable" solid objects (such as a small ball and a huge ball), the cut pieces of either one can be reassembled into the other. This is often stated informally as "a pea can be chopped up and reassembled into the Sun" and called the "pea and the Sun paradox".

The theorem is called a paradox because it contradicts basic geometric intuition. "Doubling the ball" by dividing it into parts and moving them around by rotations and translations, without any stretching, bending, or adding new points, seems to be impossible, since all these operations ought, intuitively speaking, to preserve the volume. The intuition that such operations preserve volumes is not mathematically absurd and it is even included in the formal definition of volumes. However, this is not applicable here because in this case it is impossible to define the volumes of the considered subsets. Reassembling them reproduces a set that has a volume, which happens to be different from the volume at the start.

Unlike most theorems in geometry, the mathematical proof of this result depends on the choice of axioms for set theory in a critical way. It can be proven using the axiom of choice, which allows for the construction of non-measurable sets, i.e., collections of points that do not have a volume in the ordinary sense, and whose construction requires an uncountable number of choices.

Half-Life 2: Deathmatch attempts to translate the mechanics of Half-Life 2 into a multiplayer setting, creating a somewhat enjoyable arena shooter with lots of ways to defeat your foes. It can be very satisfying at times to break all your opponent's bones by launching physics objects at them, vaporize them using a Combine energy ball, outsmart them with a well-placed tripmine, or simply bludgeon them to death with a crowbar. Despite this seeming variety in gameplay, the game can feel quite basic in terms of mechanics and tactical options, creating an experience that feels perhaps too generic compared to its more modern contemporaries, especially with the lack of advanced movement tech (rocket jumping, bhopping) present in other, similar games. Half-Life 2's simple gunplay worked well in my opinion due to the purposefully-built level design and enemy encounters, but without most of that, Half-Life 2: Deathmatch feels more like a tacked-on multiplayer mode rather than a fully fledged game that stands on its own merits. The long tradition of Source Engine modding has allowed for lots of custom maps to be created, bringing some much needed spice to the gameplay, but unless you want to die repeatedly to people from across the map seconds after spawning, I would recommend staying away from many of the servers that host mostly custom content. Despite these issues, the game is still fun to play from time to time, which is what really counts, and can be a rewarding experience if you're able to get to the same skill level as the small, established playerbase.

It does use many features such as achievements, detailed statistics, multiplayer management via the steam community, steam cloud and what else there may be.

If you want to go through a 100 hour training montage of mtn dew, sweat, and carpel tunnel to enter the base competence level for the game, and that kind of thing excited you, then go for it. As for me, I'll give it a pass.

I would not recommend this game to new players. The game kind of seems to want to be good to new players, and you can see some gestures in that direction. For example, there are short tutorial scenarios you can play through to familiarize yourself with various parts of what admittedly looks like it could be a fun and somewhat complex game. On the other hand, if someone played with some friends in custom matches, I bet that could be a really good time.

However, I would not recommend this game to someone coming in without friends already playing the game. The matchmaking system doesn't do a very good job of matching you up with other new players, which was what I had kind of been hoping for in what I had heard was a well populated moba. I remember playing Dota2 back in the day, when you would go to a LAN party to play a custom wacraft III map, and though it would be fun to revisit.

And it is kind of fun. But not really for a solo newcomer. After working my way through the tutorial, I tried out some of the "New Player" games - which it turns out means that after waiting for 1-2 minutes in matchmaking, you fight bots. I guess there aren't that many people playing when I am active, since I don't think I played a single human in those "New Player" games.

In the unranked games with other humans, I think I did OK in some games, carrying my weight. But there were a lot of games which just involved getting carried by a more experienced player, or seeing the same thing happen for the other team. When it does match making for low mmr players, the algorithm kind of throws its hands up in the air after 3-4 minutes. So you get matched with whoever, and it doesn't really make for a fun match for me as a newcomer. 

But I guess this is considered acceptable in the culture around this game, as new players are more or less expected to deal with problematic matches until they 'git gud', or maybe play a lot of matches against bots. My first hint that the game was expecting more than I was willing to give was the 100 hour requirement to enter ranked games. I'm sure a person could play against bots or go through the grind of playing in many games where they are carried by veterans to wins or losses, but that experience is just not that interesting to me.

Left 4 Dead 2, a game that has left me feeling thoroughly disappointed and disenchanted. In my humble opinion, this popular title fails to deliver on multiple fronts, leaving a bitter taste in my gaming palate. Allow me to elaborate on my grievances.
First and foremost, Left 4 Dead 2's greatest sin lies in its overt simplicity. The gameplay mechanics, while initially promising, quickly devolve into a monotonous cycle of repetitive actions. The lack of depth and complexity hampers any potential for engaging strategy or meaningful decision-making. It feels like a relentless onslaught of mindless hordes, devoid of any true challenge or intellectual stimulation.
Furthermore, my discontent grows as I examine the character selection. One cannot help but be dismayed by the inclusion of Coach, a character who seems to dominate a disproportionate share of the roster. The lack of diversity and variety in character choices is a disheartening oversight, further exacerbating the game's already overwhelming sense of tedium.
As I traversed the game's bleak environments, the visuals failed to captivate or impress. The graphics, while serviceable, lack the polish and finesse that one would expect from a modern title. The unremarkable textures and uninspired level design contribute to an overall lackluster visual experience, further diminishing any potential enjoyment that could be derived from the game.
Moreover, Left 4 Dead 2's narrative fails to engross or engage. The shallow plot, if it can even be called that, provides little motivation or incentive to continue playing. The absence of compelling character arcs or a gripping storyline leaves players adrift in a sea of indifference, detached from the game's world and its inhabitants.
In conclusion, Left 4 Dead 2 falls short of expectations on multiple fronts, rendering it an unremarkable and unenjoyable experience. Its simplistic gameplay, lack of character diversity, underwhelming visuals, and uninspired narrative collectively contribute to a lackluster affair. I find it difficult to recommend this game to anyone seeking a satisfying and engaging zombie survival experience. It is a lackluster addition to the genre, leaving much to be desired in terms of depth, innovation, and overall enjoyment.

portal 2 is a masterclass in game design , combining mind-bending puzzles , sharp wit , and a quirky narrative that keeps players engaged from start to finish . if you thought the original portal was groundbreaking , this sequel cranks everything up to eleven , delivering an experience that ’ s as challenging as it is delightful . it ’ s a game that proves that sometimes , the best way to solve problems is to think outside the box—or in this case , to create a portal and step through to the other side . at its core , portal 2 builds upon the unique mechanics of its predecessor , giving you the ability to create portals to traverse the environment and solve intricate puzzles . the introduction of new elements , like the repulsion and propulsion gels , adds even more complexity to the gameplay , ensuring that no two puzzles feel the same . each test chamber feels like a mini adventure , with ingenious designs that require you to think creatively and experiment with the game ’ s mechanics . you ’ ll find yourself saying , “ i can ’ t believe i didn ’ t see that ! ” more times than you can count . what truly sets portal 2 apart , however , is its fantastic narrative and character development . you ’ ll reunite with the sarcastic ai companion , glados , whose dark humor and cutting remarks will have you chuckling even as you try to escape her deadly traps . the addition of new characters , like the bubbling yet lovable wheatley , injects even more personality into the game . their interactions add depth to the story and keep you entertained as you navigate the dilapidated aperture science facility . the voice acting is top-notch , with standout performances that bring glados and wheatley to life in a way that ’ s both hilarious and memorable . the writing is sharp and filled with clever one-liners , making the experience feel like you ’ re part of a darkly comedic sci-fi film . you ’ ll find yourself laughing out loud as you uncover the backstory of aperture science , complete with absurd corporate jargon and bizarre experiments that are just begging to be explored . visually , portal 2 is stunning , with a stylized art direction that perfectly complements its atmosphere . the environments are richly detailed , showcasing the decay of the facility and the remnants of its quirky experiments . the sound design also deserves a mention ; from the hauntingly beautiful score to the sound effects of the portals , every audio element enhances the immersive experience . one of the best parts of portal 2 is its replayability . after you ’ ve completed the main campaign , the co-op mode opens up a whole new realm of challenges that require teamwork and coordination with a friend . solving puzzles together while relying on each other ’ s skills adds another layer of enjoyment and proves that portals are even more fun with a buddy . in conclusion , portal 2 is not just a game ; it ’ s an experience that captivates you with its clever puzzles , engaging storytelling , and delightful humor . whether you ’ re a seasoned gamer or someone new to the world of puzzle games , portal 2 offers something for everyone . so grab your portal gun , prepare for some brain-bending fun , and get ready to dive into a world where the only limit is your imagination ( and maybe a few clever portals ) !

Half-Life Blue Shift is the second expansion to the original Half-Life.  Although it doesn't carry over some the new elements that Opposing Force introduced (sniper rifles, night vision goggles), it is justifiable considering the context of the game's story (though it is disappointing).  You play as Barney Calhoun, a security officer working in Black Mesa during the incident.  Similar to the original, your goal is to escape Black Mesa while fighting aliens and military forces.  I particularly enjoyed sections where you worked with the Black Mesa scientists to escape, making the characters and interactions with them feel more authentic to a more cinematic experience.  Sadly, there is no online mode in this game (unlike the original game and Opposing Force), so I can only recommend this game if you are invested in the Half-Life story like me.
